title: On learning in the presence of biased data and strategic behavior
speaker: Avrim Blum
abstract: In this talk I will discuss two lines of work involving learning in the presence of biased data and strategic behavior. In the first, we ask whether fairness constraints on learning algorithms can actually improve the accuracy of the classifier produced, when training data is unrepresentative or corrupted due to bias. Typically, fairness constraints are analyzed as a tradeoff with classical objectives such as accuracy. Our results here show there are natural scenarios where they can be a win-win, helping to improve overall accuracy.

proof_stages.label: Fairness criteria as accuracy constraints under bias
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates the question of whether fairness constraints, normally viewed as trading off with accuracy, can improve accuracy when the training distribution is biased, and reviews equality of opportunity, equalized odds, calibration, and demographic parity.

proof_stages.label: Stylized biased-data and ground-truth model
proof_stages.summary: The first model fixes two observable groups, ERM in a large-sample limit, underrepresentation and labeling bias mechanisms for the disadvantaged group, and Bayes-optimal group classifiers whose random-classification-noise structure makes them satisfy the fairness notions on the unbiased distribution.

proof_stages.label: Underrepresentation-bias analysis
proof_stages.summary: When group-B positives are underrepresented, unconstrained ERM may eventually classify all of group B as negative, calibration and demographic parity move boundaries in harmful or accuracy-reducing ways, while equality of opportunity/equalized odds keep the Bayes-optimal classifiers feasible and effectively lock them in.

proof_stages.label: Labeling-bias and combined-bias comparison
proof_stages.summary: For mislabeled group-B positives, equality of opportunity still protects the Bayes-optimal classifier, demographic parity can also work in the pure labeling case, equalized odds fails because the apparent negative distribution is distorted, and calibration again compensates in the wrong direction; with both biases, equality of opportunity remains the robust notion in the stylized model.

proof_stages.label: Strategic online classification model and naive-algorithm failure
proof_stages.summary: The second part sets up online binary classification with agents who know the current rule and can pay movement costs to be labeled positive, relates this to strategic measurement and prior work, and shows by a perceptron cycling example that standard online linear-classification algorithms can fail.

proof_stages.label: Manipulation-aware perceptron for known Euclidean costs
proof_stages.summary: Assuming a known manipulation radius and an underlying perfect linear separator, the algorithm shifts the current hyperplane by the manipulation radius and updates only with reconstructed vectors whose dot product is positive with the true separator and nonpositive with the current hypothesis.

proof_stages.label: Extensions, limitations, and open strategic variants
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains how the approach extends to known weighted l1 costs, notes that unknown costs currently lead to exponential grid searches, and discusses in Q&A partial observability, heterogeneous manipulation, probabilistic rules, and related uncertain-agent models.
